title: Differentiation
summary: Free Cambridge 9709 Pure Maths 1 lesson on differentiation — power rule, tangents, stationary points, and how examiners award marks.

sections.content: Differentiation is the engine of **9709 Pure Paper 1**. You will differentiate polynomials, find gradients of tangents, and classify stationary points. Examiners often bundle two skills in one question — differentiate correctly **and** interpret what the derivative means.
If y = x^n then dy/dx = n x^(n-1). Constant multiples and sums differentiate term by term.

items: Bring the power down, reduce the power by 1
dy/dx at a point = gradient of the tangent to the curve
Stationary points: set dy/dx = 0 and solve
Second derivative (or sign table) tells you max vs min

sections.content: When dy/dx = 0 you get turning points. Cambridge expects you to **find coordinates** (substitute x back into y) and **state the nature** (maximum, minimum, or point of inflection). A sign table of dy/dx around the point is acceptable if second derivative is messy.

sections.question: y = x³ − 3x² + 2. Find stationary points and their nature.
sections.solution:
dy/dx = 3x² − 6x = 3x(x − 2). Stationary when x = 0 or x = 2.

When x = 0, y = 2 → (0, 2). When x = 2, y = 8 − 12 + 2 = −2 → (2, −2).

d²y/dx² = 6x − 6. At x = 0: −6 (max). At x = 2: +6 (min).

sections.content: Show **dy/dx** clearly before solving = 0. Method marks (M1) are lost when candidates jump to coordinates with no derivative shown. Use exact fractions unless the paper says otherwise.

sections.label: Mark a differentiation question on MarkScheme
